title: Panchatantra Moral Fables Chapter 5 (Full Canonical Coverage — Book 5 Completion)
revision_note: Batch 5 (2026-07-23): Completes Book V (Ill-Considered Action) to 11/11 canonical inserted tales, as part of the full-82-story canonical coverage expansion (see docs/PANCHATANTRA_CANONICAL_SOURCE_PLAN.md). 3 stories were already ingested in Batch 3 (The Loyal Mungoose, The Lion-Makers, The Brahman's Dream); this batch adds the remaining 8, all NEW to both the reader (src/lib/katha-library.ts) and the AI corpus: The Four Treasure-Seekers, Hundred-Wit/Thousand-Wit/Single-Wit, The Musical Donkey, Slow the Weaver, The Unforgiving Monkey, The Credulous Fiend, The Three-Breasted Princess, and The Fiend Who Washed His Feet. Source: shreevatsa.net/ryder/old/dfnp/2009-restart/ryder.html (Ryder 1925/Hertel recension HTML export) -- the same Book 5 source and rights basis established in Batch 3 (Ryder's 1925 US copyright, renewed 1953, expired per the page's own copyright-law analysis; Ryder died 1938, public domain for decades in life+70 countries including India). Note on 'The Three-Breasted Princess': Ryder's original includes a plot point involving an extramarital relationship and an attempted murder-by-poisoning; the curated_lesson paraphrase in this manifest and the reader entry describe this as a conspiracy/betrayal without explicit detail, consistent with the app's general-audience tone, while the verbatim 'text' field preserves Ryder's actual translation for source-fidelity purposes. Book V is now 100% covered (11/11) in both the reader and the AI corpus.

content.text:
In a certain town in the world were four Brahmans who lived as the best of friends. And being stricken with utter poverty, they took counsel together... "Let us at any cost strive to make money." ... So the magician [Terror-Joy] made four magic quills, and gave one to each, saying: "Go to the northern slope of the Himalaya Mountains. And wherever a quill drops, there the owner will certainly find a treasure."

Now as they followed his directions, the leader's quill dropped. And on examining the spot, he found the soil all copper. So he said: "Look here! Take all the copper you want." But the others said: "Fool! What is the good of a thing which, even in quantity, does not put an end to poverty? Stand up. Let us go on." And he replied: "You may go. I will accompany you no farther." So he took his copper and was the first to turn back.

The three others went farther. But they had traveled only a little way when the leader's quill dropped. And when he dug down, he found the soil all silver. At this he was delighted... "You two may go," said he. "I will not join you." So he took his silver and turned back.

The two went on until one quill dropped. When the owner dug down, he found the soil all gold. Seeing this, he was delighted... "Fool!" said the other. "Don't you see the point? First came copper, then silver, and then gold. Beyond there will certainly be gems. Stand up. Let us go farther." "You may go," he replied. "I will stay here and wait for you."

So the other went on alone. His limbs were scorched by the rays of the summer sun and his thoughts were confused by thirst... At last... he saw a man with blood dripping down his body; for a wheel was whirling on his head. Then he made haste and said: "Sir, why do you stand thus with a wheel whirling on your head? ...tell me if there is water anywhere. I am mad with thirst."

The moment the Brahman said this, the wheel left the other's head and settled on his own... And the fellow said: "When someone who holds in his hand a magic quill such as you had, arrives and speaks as you did, then it will settle on his head."

"My dear sir," said the fellow, "the god of wealth, fearful lest his treasures be stolen, prepared this terror, so that no magician might come so far. And if any should succeed in coming, he was to be freed from hunger and thirst, preserved from decrepitude and death, and was merely to endure this torture. So now permit me to say farewell. You have set me free from a sizable misery. Now I am going home." And he went.
content.curated_lesson: Four Brahman friends, worn down by poverty, resolved to seek their fortune abroad. A wandering magician gave each of them a magic quill and told them to walk toward the Himalaya: wherever a man's quill dropped from his hand, treasure would be found beneath that very spot. The first man's quill fell where the soil was pure copper. Satisfied, he filled his bag and turned back, content with what he had. Walking on, the second man's quill fell over silver -- surely richer than copper -- and he too turned home, pleased with his gain. The third man's quill dropped over solid gold. His companion urged him onward, reasoning that if copper led to silver and silver to gold, something even greater must lie ahead. But the gold-finder had already found enough, and turned back satisfied, letting his friend go on alone. The last man pressed on alone through the scorching land until he found not gems, as he had hoped, but a man imprisoned by a whirling wheel of torment, cursed to bear it forever -- because he too had once asked the same greedy question the wrong way, without knowing when enough was enough. The moment he asked, the wheel leapt from that stranger's head onto his own, and only then did he understand the price of chasing "just a little more" without limit.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text:
In a certain pond lived two fishes whose names were Hundred-Wit and Thousand-Wit. And a frog named Single-Wit made friends with them.

One day at sunset they were engaged in conversation, when fishermen with nets came there, who said to one another on seeing the pond: "Look! This pond appears to contain plenty of fish... We will return at dawn." With this they went home.

The three friends felt this speech to be dreadful as the fall of a thunderbolt... The frog spoke first: "Hundred-Wit and Thousand-Wit, my dear friends, what should we do now: flee or stick it out?"

At this Thousand-wit laughed and said: "My good friend, do not be frightened merely because you have heard words... Yet should it take place, I will save you and myself by virtue of my wit. For I know plenty of tricks in the water." And Hundred-Wit added: "Yes, Thousand-Wit is quite right... You must not go away. I will save you by virtue of my wit."

"Well," said the frog, "I have only a single wit, and that tells me to flee. My wife and I are going to some other body of water this very night." So spoke the frog and under cover of night he went to another body of water.

At dawn the next day came the fish-catchers... and inclosed the pond with nets. And all the fishes, turtles, frogs, crabs, and other water-creatures were caught in the nets and captured. Even Hundred-Wit and Thousand-Wit fell into a net and were killed, though they struggled to save their lives by fancy turns.

On the following day the fishermen gleefully started home. One of them carried Hundred-Wit, who was heavy, on his head. Another carried Thousand-Wit tied to a cord. Then the frog, safe in the throat of a cistern, said to his wife: "Look, darling, look! While Hundred-Wit is on a head, While Thousand-Wit hangs limp and dead, Your humble Single-Wit, my dear, Is paddling in the water clear.
content.curated_lesson: In a pond lived two fish named Hundred-Wit and Thousand-Wit, along with their friend, a frog named Single-Wit. One evening they overheard fishermen planning to return at dawn with nets. Alarmed, the frog asked his friends whether they should flee that very night. Thousand-Wit laughed off the danger, boasting that his thousand tricks would surely save them. Hundred-Wit agreed, confident that a hundred clever schemes were more than enough. The frog admitted he had only a single idea, and it was simply to leave at once -- so that night, he and his wife swam away to another pond. At dawn the fishermen returned and swept the pond with their nets. Both clever fish, for all their boasted wit, were caught and carried away. From the safety of his new home, the frog watched the fishermen pass by carrying his two brilliant friends, and said quietly to his wife that a single wit, acted on in time, had outlasted a thousand clever tricks that were never used.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text:
In a certain town was a donkey named Prig. In the daytime he carried laundry packages, but was at liberty to wander anywhere at night. One night while wandering in the fields he fell in with a jackal and made friends. So the two broke through a hedge into cucumber-beds, and having eaten what they could hold of that comestible, parted at dawn to go home.

One night the egotistical donkey, standing among the cucumbers, said to the jackal: "See, nephew! The night is marvelously fine. I will contribute a song." "Don't, uncle," said the jackal. "It might make trouble, seeing that we are on thieves' business... Besides, your vocal music is not agreeable, since it resembles a blast on a conch-shell. The farmers would hear you from afar, would rise, and would fetter or kill you. Better keep quiet and eat."

"Come, come!" said the donkey. "Your remarks prove that you live in the woods and have no musical taste..." [The donkey recites an elaborate technical description of music theory to prove his qualifications.] "After this, how can you think me lacking in educated taste? How can you try to hinder me?"

"Very well, uncle," said the jackal. "I will stay by the gap in the hedge, and look for farmers. You may sing to heart's content." When he had done so, the donkey lifted his neck and began to utter sounds. But the farmers, hearing the bray of a donkey, angrily clenched their teeth, snatched cudgels, rushed in, and beat him so that he fell to the ground. Next they hobbled him by fastening on his neck a mortar with a convenient hole, then went to sleep.

Presently the donkey stood up... Then with the mortar on his neck, he trampled the hedge and started to run away. At this moment the jackal, looking on from a safe distance, said with a smile: "Well sung, uncle! Why would you Not stop when I told you to? What a necklace! Yes, you wear Music medals rich and rare." "Just so, you would not stop when I advised it.
content.curated_lesson: A donkey and a jackal, friends by night, would slip through a gap in the hedge into a farmer's cucumber field and feast together, always parting before dawn. One fine night, the donkey -- proud of his voice -- announced that he wished to sing. The jackal begged him to stay quiet. The donkey, offended at being called unmusical, launched into a long, proud explanation of the rules of classical music, insisting he was too cultured to be silenced. The jackal quietly stepped away to keep watch -- and the donkey brayed loudly across the field, exactly as the jackal had feared. The farmers woke at once, rushed out with clubs, beat the donkey soundly, and fastened a heavy mortar around his neck. As the battered donkey dragged himself away, the jackal called out with a smile: he had been offered fine advice and refused it, and now wore quite a "necklace" for his singing.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text:
In a certain town lived a weaver named Slow. One day all the pegs in his loom broke. So he took an axe, and in his search for wood, came to the seashore. There he found a great sissoo tree, and he thought: "This seems a good-sized tree. If I cut it down, I can make plenty of weaving-tools." He therefore lifted his axe upon it.

Now there was a fairy in the tree who said: "My friend, this tree is my home. Please spare it..." "Sir," said the fairy, "I have taken a liking to you. Ask anything you like, but spare this tree." "In that case," said the weaver, "I will go home and return after asking my friend and my wife."

...he encountered his particular friend, the barber, and said: "My friend, I have won the favor of a fairy. Tell me what to ask for." And the barber said: "My dear fellow... ask for a kingdom. You can be king, and I will be prime minister..." "Quite so, my friend," replied the weaver. "However, I shall ask my wife, too." "Don't," said the barber. "It is a mistake to consult women..."

And the weaver rejoined: "You may be right. Still, I shall ask her. She is a good wife." ...he said to her: "...I have come to ask you to tell me what to say to him. Here is my friend, the barber, who tells me to ask for a kingdom." "Dear husband," said she, "what sense have barbers? Do not take his advice... this king-business means a series of dreadful troubles..."

"Yes," said the weaver, "you are right. But tell me what to ask for." And she replied: "As it is, you turn out one piece of cloth a day, and this meets all our expenses. Now ask for a second pair of arms and an extra head, so that you may produce one piece of cloth in front and another behind. The price of one meets the household expenses, with the price of the other you may put on style..."

...he was delighted and said: "Splendid, my faithful wife! You have made a splendid suggestion. I am determined to follow it." So the weaver went and laid his request before the fairy: "...pray give me a second pair of arms and an extra head." And in the act of speaking he became two-headed and four-armed.

But as he came home, delight in his heart, the people thought he was a fiend, and beat him with clubs and stones and things so that he died.
content.curated_lesson: A weaver named Slow, needing wood for his broken loom, raised his axe against a great tree by the sea -- only to discover it was home to a forest spirit, who begged him to spare it in exchange for any wish he desired. Before deciding, Slow consulted his friend the barber, who urged him to ask for a kingdom. But Slow insisted on asking his wife too. His wife thought the barber's idea foolish -- a kingdom brings only worry and rivalry. Instead she suggested that since one pair of hands already wove enough cloth to support them, an extra head and pair of arms would let him weave twice as much, doubling their income without any of a king's troubles. Delighted with this "sensible" plan, Slow asked the spirit for exactly that -- and instantly became two-headed and four-armed. But as he walked home rejoicing, the townspeople saw only a monstrous figure approaching and, terrified, beat him to death before he could explain himself.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text:
In a certain city was a king named Moon, who had a pack of monkeys for his son's amusement... For the amusement of the same prince there was a herd of rams. One of them had an itching tongue, so he went into the kitchen at all hours of the day and night and swallowed everything in sight. And the cooks would beat him with any stick or other object within reach.

Now when the chief of the monkeys observed this, he reflected: "Dear me! This quarrel between ram and cooks will mean the destruction of the monkeys... Suppose some time they find nothing else and beat him with a firebrand. Then that broad, woolly back will very easily catch fire. And if the ram, while burning, plunges into the stable near by, it will blaze... and the horses will be scorched. Now the standard work on veterinary science prescribes monkey-fat to relieve burns on horses..." Having reached this conclusion, he assembled the monkeys [and urged them to flee to the forest].

But the conceited monkeys laughed at his warning... Having listened to this, the monkey chief... said: "...I will not behold the death of my household. I am off for that very forest." With these words the chief left them all behind, and went to the forest.

One day after he had gone, the ram entered the kitchen. And the cook, finding nothing else, picked up a firebrand, half-consumed and still blazing, and struck him. Whereat, with half his body blazing, he plunged bleating into the stable near by. There he rolled until flames started up on all sides... and of the horses tethered there some died... In this state of affairs, the saddened king assembled the veterinary surgeons... they said: "...Let monkey-fat be freely used..." When the king heard this, he ordered the slaughter of the monkeys. And, not to waste words, every one was killed.

Now the monkey chief did not with his own eyes see this outrage... But he heard the story as it passed from one to another, and did not take it tamely... Now as the elderly monkey wandered about thirsty, he came to a lake... he noticed footprints leading into the lake, but none coming out... there issued from the water a man-eating fiend with a pearl necklace adorning his neck, who spoke and said: "Sir, I eat everyone who enters the water... I have taken a liking to you. Name your heart's desire."

"And I," said the monkey, "I live in mortal enmity with a king. If you will give me that pearl necklace, I will awaken his greed with a plausible narrative, and will make that king enter the lake along with his retinue." So the fiend handed over the pearl necklace.

[The monkey then lures the king and his court to the lake with tales of a magic pearl-granting lake.] So the king and his retinue started, greedy for pearl necklaces... So all the attendants entered and were eaten by the fiend. Then, as they lingered, the king said to the monkey: "Well, chief, why do my attendants linger?" And the monkey hurriedly climbed a tree before saying to the king: "You villainous king, your attendants are eaten by a fiend that lives in the water. My enmity with you, arising from the death of my household, has been brought to a happy termination. Now go. I did not make you enter there, because I remembered that you were the king..."

When the king heard this, he hastened home, grief-stricken.
content.curated_lesson: A king kept a troupe of pet monkeys for his son's amusement, alongside a herd of rams. One greedy ram kept raiding the palace kitchen, and the cooks regularly drove him off. The eldest monkey saw the danger: if the cooks ever struck the ram with a burning stick, he might catch fire, stampede into the stable, and burn the horses -- and monkey-fat was the prescribed remedy for burned horses. He urged the troupe to leave for the forest at once, but the others laughed at his fear and refused. Unwilling to watch his family come to harm, the old monkey left alone. Soon after, exactly as he had feared, the burning ram fled into the stable, the horses were burned, and the king's physicians prescribed monkey-fat -- so every monkey left behind was killed. When the old monkey learned what had happened, he found a lake guarded by a man-eating spirit and struck a bargain: in exchange for a pearl necklace, he lured the same king and his retinue to the lake with tales of treasure, letting the spirit take them as they entered the water, while sparing the king himself with a final warning about what his cruelty had cost.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text:
In a certain city lived a king whose name was Fine-Army. He had a daughter named Pearl... Now a certain fiend, who wished to carry her off, came every evening and abused her, but he could not carry her off because she protected herself by drawing a magic circle.

While matters were in this state, the fiend once... revealed himself to the princess, who thereupon said to a girl friend: "Look, my dear! This is the fiend who comes every evening at twilight's hour and torments me. Is there any means of keeping the ruffian at a distance?"

When he heard this, the fiend thought: "Aha! I am not the only one. There is someone else -- and his name is Twilight -- who comes every day to carry her off. But he cannot do it either. Suppose I take the form of a horse, go to the stable, and find out what he looks like and what power he has."

When he had done so, a horse-thief came to the palace at dead of night. He examined all the horses, found the fiend-horse the finest, put a bit in his mouth, and mounted. Meanwhile the fiend was thinking: "I presume this is the fellow named Twilight. He thinks me a vile creature, he is angry, he has come to kill me. What shall I do?"

While he was thinking, the horse-thief struck him with a whip. And he was terrified and started to run. The thief, for his part, after traveling some distance, tried to stop him by tugging at the bit... he reflected: "Well, well! Horses are not like this. This must be a fiend in equine form. So if I find a spot thick with dust, I will drop. It is my one chance of life."

...the fiend-horse passed under a banyan tree. And the thief caught a branch and stuck. So both of them gained the hope of life from their separation... Now in the banyan was a monkey, a friend of the fiend, who said when he saw the fiend making off: "Look here! Why do you run from an imaginary danger? This is your natural food, a man. Eat him."

On hearing this, the fiend took his own form and turned about... And when the thief saw that the monkey had called him back, he was angry. As the monkey sat above, and his tail hung down, the thief took it in his mouth and started to chew very hard. Then the monkey concluded that he was dealing with one more powerful than the fiend, and was too frightened to utter a word.
content.curated_lesson: A fiend haunted a princess every evening, unable to carry her off because she protected herself with a magic circle. One night, overhearing her complain about "the one who torments me every evening at twilight," the fiend mistook this for the name of a rival -- someone called Twilight, more powerful than himself. Anxious to learn what this rival looked like, the fiend took the form of a horse and slipped into the royal stables. That night, a horse-thief crept in, chose the disguised fiend, and rode off on him. Every tug of the reins convinced the terrified fiend that "Twilight" himself was punishing him. He bolted in panic, while the thief, equally alarmed that this horse obeyed no rein, clung on until he could grab a tree branch and escape. A monkey friend of the fiend mocked him for running from a mere man, and the fiend, embarrassed, turned back to attack -- but the thief, now furious, seized the monkey's dangling tail in his teeth and bit down hard, and the monkey, mistaking this for an even more terrifying force, froze in silent terror.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.text:
In the north country was a city called Honey-Town, where the king was named Honey-Host. And once there was born to him a daughter with three breasts. As soon as he learned of the birth of a three-breasted girl, he summoned the chamberlain and said: "Sir, let this girl be exposed in the forest, so that not a single soul may learn the fact."

To this the chamberlain replied: "O king of kings, it is a well-known fact that a three-breasted daughter brings misfortune. In spite of this, the Brahmans should be summoned and their opinion asked, in order that no law be offended..." [The chamberlain illustrates this principle with the embedded tale "The Fiend Who Washed His Feet."]

After listening to this, the king summoned the Brahmans... And they replied: "O King, listen. A daughter fitted out with limbs Too numerous or few, Will lose her character... But if the father sees a girl With triple breast about, She dooms him to a speedy death... Therefore, O King, shun the sight of her. Give her to anyone who will marry her, but banish him from the country."

...he ordered a proclamation... "There is a three-breasted princess. To anyone who marries her the king will give a hundred thousand gold-pieces, but will exile him." For a long time this proclamation was made without anyone marrying the princess...

Now there was a blind man in the city, and as companion he had a hunchback named Slow, who guided him with a staff. These two heard the drum and consulted... "In case we touch that drum, we get girl and gold... even if death results from the girl's deformity, it will put a final end to the wretchedness of poverty."

...the blind man went and touched the drum, saying: "I will marry the girl." ...the king's men... took the three-breasted princess to the river-bank, married her to the blind man, and gave him the hundred thousand gold-pieces... [they were sent] to a foreign land.

...There in a certain town they purchased a house and lived comfortably. The blind man, however, spent all his time dozing on a couch. The hunchback did the housework. In course of time the princess had an intrigue with the hunchback, and she said: "My beloved, if this blind man happened to be killed, we should live happily together. Please find some poison..."

...the hunchback picked up a dead black snake... "Please cut it up, season it... and give it to that eyeless fellow, telling him it is fish. Then he will die in a hurry." ...she cut up the snake, put it in a kettle with buttermilk, placed it over the fire... she civilly said to the blind man: "...please take the spoon and stir them." ...he was delighted... took the spoon, and started to stir.

But as he did so, the poisoned steam reached his eyes, and the film began to peel off... his vision cleared and looking into the kettle, he saw nothing but chopped black snake... he concealed his feelings and behaved like a blind man. Presently Slow returned and without hesitation began to hug the wife... And the blind man saw it all.

Not finding a knife, he went up to Slow in the old way, wrathfully seized him by the feet, whirled him about his head with every bit of strength he could muster, and dashed him against the chest of the three-breasted woman. And the blow from the hunchback's body forced the third breast in, while the hunchback, when his hump smashed against her bosom, became straight.
content.curated_lesson: A king's newborn daughter carried a rare mark that the customs of the land considered a grave misfortune. His counselor advised consulting scholars, who ruled that she must be married off to whoever would have her, and both then exiled. A drum was sounded announcing gold and a bride to anyone willing to marry her and leave. A blind man, guided by a hunchbacked companion, accepted the offer for the gold, reasoning that even hardship was better than the poverty they knew. The three settled in a distant town. Over time, resentment grew between the hunchback and the princess against the blind man who depended so heavily on them, and the two began plotting to be rid of him. Their scheme unraveled in confusion: in the middle of carrying it out, the blind man's sight was accidentally restored by fumes he had not expected, and in the struggle that followed, the impact that was meant to harm him instead straightened the hunchback's back and smoothed away the mark the princess had carried since birth. What was set in motion as betrayal ended, by chance, correcting every one of the misfortunes that had brought the three of them together in the first place.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)

content.story_title: The Fiend Who Washed His Feet
content.text:
In a certain forest lived a fiend named Cruel. One day he met a Brahman in his wanderings, climbed on his shoulder, and said: "Now go ahead."

So the terrified Brahman started off with him. But on observing that the fiend's feet were soft as a lotus-heart, he asked him: "Sir, why are your feet so tender?" And the fiend replied: "I am under a vow never to touch the ground with my feet until I have washed them."

Soon the Brahman, while meditating a plan of escape, came to a lake. Here the fiend said: "Sir, do not stir from this spot until I come forth from the lake after bathing and worshiping the god."

Thereupon the Brahman thought: "He will be sure to eat me after his worship. I will hurry away. For he will not follow me with unwashen feet." And when he did so, the fiend, not daring to break his vow, did not follow.

"And that is why I say: A prudent man should always ask, and the rest of it.
content.curated_lesson: A fiend seized a Brahman in the forest, climbed onto his shoulders, and ordered him to walk on. The frightened Brahman noticed the fiend's feet were unusually soft. When asked why, the fiend explained he was bound by a vow never to let his feet touch the ground unwashed. They came to a lake, and the fiend told the Brahman to wait while he bathed and completed his worship. The Brahman understood what awaited him once the fiend returned, and also understood that, bound by his own vow, the fiend would not dare set his unwashed feet on the ground to chase him. So the moment the fiend entered the water, the Brahman ran for his life -- and true to his word, the fiend did not follow. The very discipline the fiend had imposed on himself became the one weakness that set his intended victim free.
content.source_name: The Panchatantra, translated by Arthur W. Ryder (1925)
